source_pdf: Your Complete Mobility Guide_ A Simple Guide to Mobility Aids _ Southern.pdf
source_summary: Mobility aid guide covering canes, crutches, manual wheelchairs, power wheelchairs, scooters, walkers, and rollators.
medical_safety_note: This JSON is an accessibility/life-impact catalogue derived from the attached PDF and cautious general health-literacy reasoning. It is not a diagnostic tool, treatment plan, or medical advice. Individual impact varies, especially with comorbid conditions, environment, support, money, housing, work demands, flare cycles, and care access.

entry_schema.id: stable slug unique within this JSON
entry_schema.name: condition, limitation, concept, or mobility aid name
entry_schema.source_group: category used by the source PDF
entry_schema.source_page: first page where the source term appears, when found
entry_schema.source_excerpt: short extracted context from the PDF, when available
entry_schema.life_limitations: ways it may limit daily living, relationships, work, safety, mobility, communication, or energy
entry_schema.possible_life_improvements_or_supports: ways recognition, accommodations, treatment, tools, community, or the aid itself may slightly improve life/functioning
entry_schema.support_or_accommodation_ideas: non-prescriptive supports to discuss with appropriate professionals/support people
entry_schema.spoon_energy_notes: how it may affect planning under Spoon Theory / energy pacing
entry_schema.individual_variability_note: reminder that impact differs by person and day

source_specific_note: The source emphasizes choosing aids based on individual needs and advice from clinicians/experts where appropriate.

entries.name: Mobility aid/device
entries.source_excerpt: MOBILITY GUIDE Home / Resources / Mobility Guide MOBILITY DEVICES Canes Crutches Manual Wheelchairs Power Wheelchairs Scooters Walkers Rollators MOBILITY MATTERS: YOUR GUIDE TO MOBILITY AIDS AND MORE    6/12/26, 5:32 AM Your Complete Mobility Guide: A Simple Guide to Mobility Aids | Southern https://www.spchme.com/resources/mobility-guide 1/16
life_limitations: May require learning, fit/measurement, maintenance, storage, transportation space, accessible routes, and social confidence to use well.
Can be limited by stairs, narrow doorways, uneven ground, snow/ice, inaccessible bathrooms, crowded spaces, or stigma.
possible_life_improvements_or_supports: Can improve safety, independence, comfort, confidence, and participation by reducing falls, pain, fatigue, or distance barriers.
Can conserve spoons by making movement less physically expensive.
entries.spoon_energy_notes: Usually reduces mobility spoon cost when matched well; may add setup/transportation spoon cost.
entries.individual_variability_note: Impact can be mild, moderate, severe, intermittent, progressive, or invisible depending on the person and context.
entries.device_description: device designed to help people with problems moving around complete daily activities and increase freedom/independence

entries.name: Cane

life_limitations: May require learning, fit/measurement, maintenance, storage, transportation space, accessible routes, and social confidence to use well.
Can be limited by stairs, narrow doorways, uneven ground, snow/ice, inaccessible bathrooms, crowded spaces, or stigma.
Provides limited support compared with walkers/wheelchairs and may strain hand/wrist/shoulder if poorly fitted or overused.
possible_life_improvements_or_supports: Can improve safety, independence, comfort, confidence, and participation by reducing falls, pain, fatigue, or distance barriers.
Can conserve spoons by making movement less physically expensive.
Can widen the base of support, improve balance, reduce weight-bearing pressure, and allow shorter independent walks.
entries.spoon_energy_notes: Usually reduces mobility spoon cost when matched well; may add setup/transportation spoon cost.
entries.individual_variability_note: Impact can be mild, moderate, severe, intermittent, progressive, or invisible depending on the person and context.
entries.device_description: supports balance/stability, leg or trunk weakness, injury, chronic pain, and may reduce weight-bearing pressure

entries.name: Single-Point Cane
entries.source_excerpt: well. TYPES OF CRUTCHES AVAILABLE There are three types of crutches available: Single-Point Cane: The standard cane that's most common and has a single support point at the end of it. It's suitable for many people. ✔ Multiple-Point Cane: Also known as a quad cane and tripod cane, this type of cane has several points that touch the ground and provide more stable support for individuals who require more assistance with balancing. entries.name: Gutter Crutches / Platform Crutches
entries.source_excerpt: HOW TO USE CRUTCHES Below, we've shared some basic tips on moving around on crutches from MedlinePlus. These tips may or may not apply depending on the type of crutches you're using. Speak to your physician or physical therapist if you need help with your crutch. 1. Let your hands carry your weight and not your armpits. 2. Look forward when you're walking, not down at your feet. 3. Pick a chair with armrests to make sitting and standing easier. 4. Ensure t

entries.name: Standard Wheelchair
entries.source_excerpt: have listed some of the most common types below as defined by Sunrise Medical. Standard Wheelchair: Standard wheelchairs are the most frequently used wheelchairs. They weigh over 35 lbs. without the front rigging and have a seat width and depth between 16 and 20 inches. Standard wheelchairs will have fixed or detachable armrests and have more adjustability than a transport chair. Transport Wheelchair: A transport wheelchair may be used by an individual who doesn’t require a manual wheelchair daily. These wheelchairs are used to “tran

entries.name: Transport Wheelchair
entries.source_excerpt: ixed or detachable armrests and have more adjustability than a transport chair. Transport Wheelchair: A transport wheelchair may be used by an individual who doesn’t require a manual wheelchair daily. These wheelchairs are used to “transport” a person from one location to another when they’re unable to walk the required distance; for example, from a parked car to an office. People using these types of wheelchairs tend to be dependent on propulsion (another person pushes the wheelchair for the one that is sitting in it). Transport whee
life_limitations: May require learning, fit/measurement, maintenance, storage, transportation space, accessible routes, and social confidence to use well.
Can be limited by stairs, narrow doorways, uneven ground, snow/ice, inaccessible bathrooms, crowded spaces, or stigma.

Often depends on another person for propulsion and is not ideal for independent daily self-propelling.
possible_life_improvements_or_supports: Can improve safety, independence, comfort, confidence, and participation by reducing falls, pain, fatigue, or distance barriers.
Can conserve spoons by making movement less physically expensive.
Can make indoor/outdoor movement possible with reduced walking/standing demands and may support everyday activities despite reduced mobility.
Can make appointments/errands possible when walking distance is too much but daily wheelchair use is not needed.

entries.name: Folding Wheelchair
entries.source_excerpt: tments, which is why they aren’t ideal for someone who will be self-propelling. Folding Wheelchair: Folding wheelchairs allow for easy folding and transportation. They're lighter in weight than standard wheelchairs and have more configuration options available including wheel types, tires, casters, armrests and wheel locks. A key differentiator between folding and standard wheelchairs is that folding wheelchairs have the ability to modify the placement of the rear wheel (it can be adjusted vertically and horizontally). entries.name: Rigid Wheelchair
entries.source_excerpt: the lateral position of the rear wheel can be adjusted in a folding wheelchair. Rigid Wheelchair: Rigid wheelchairs allow for easier propulsion, and the more rigid the wheelchair, the easier it'll be to propel. A rigid wheelchair is lighter in weight than a folding wheelchair and doesn’t have a cross- brace, which helps reduce the overall weight. The placement of the rear wheels can be modified to adjust the center of gravity, rear seat-to-floor height, camber and lateral access for an individual. Optimal positioning of the rear wheel

entries.source_excerpt: TYPES OF POWER WHEELCHAIRS AVAILABLE The three main ‘types’ of power wheelchairs are rear-wheel, front-wheel and mid-wheel drive. From there, customizations are done by adding different components and accessories. These add-ons allow users to get a bespoke wheelchair that meets their needs. entries.name: Mobility Scooter
entries.source_excerpt: tions you may have! Contact an Expert Back to Top WHAT IS A SCOOTER USED FOR? A mobility scooter is a mobility aid similar to a wheelchair but configured like a motorized scooter. A mobility scooter has a seat over three, four or five wheels, foot plates for the feet, and handlebars in front to turn one, two or Two large drive wheels positioned near the front of the chair’s base. Offer smooth ride and great for obstacle climbing. Biggest wheels being at front increases the height the chair can climb. Two smaller rear casters to aid in
life_limitations: May require learning, fit/measurement, maintenance, storage, transportation space, accessible routes, and social confidence to use well.
Can be limited by stairs, narrow doorways, uneven ground, snow/ice, inaccessible bathrooms, crowded spaces, or stigma.
Requires upright posture, steering ability, hand/shoulder function, storage, charging, and enough space; not ideal for all indoor or postural-support needs.
possible_life_improvements_or_supports: Can improve safety, independence, comfort, confidence, and participation by reducing falls, pain, fatigue, or distance barriers.
Can conserve spoons by making movement less physically expensive.
Can support longer distances and community access when walking stamina is limited.
entries.spoon_energy_notes: Usually reduces mobility spoon cost when matched well; may add setup/transportation spoon cost.
entries.individual_variability_note: Impact can be mild, moderate, severe, intermittent, progressive, or invisible depending on the person and context.
entries.device_description: scooter-like mobility aid that reduces manual propulsion but requires posture, shoulder/hand strength, and upper body mobility

entries.name: Travel/Portable Scooter
entries.source_excerpt: re. TYPES OF MOBILITY SCOOTERS AVAILABLE There are three main types of scooters—travel/portable scooters and three-wheel and four-wheel scooters. Mobility scooters have various features available such as different types of tillers, seats, suspension, wheels and more. Travel/Portable Scooters: These scooters can fold or be disassembled so they can fit into the trunk of a car or as airplane cargo. These scooters are often lighter than full-sized three- or four-wheel scooters. ✔ Three-Wheel Scooters: These mobility scooters have a tight

entries.name: Walker
entries.source_excerpt: e MOBILITY DEVICES Canes Crutches Manual Wheelchairs Power Wheelchairs Scooters Walkers Rollators MOBILITY MATTERS: YOUR GUIDE TO MOBILITY AIDS AND MORE    6/12/26, 5:32 AM Your Complete Mobility Guide: A Simple Guide to Mobility Aids | Southern https://www.spchme.com/resources/mobility-guide 1/16

Can be limited by stairs, narrow doorways, uneven ground, snow/ice, inaccessible bathrooms, crowded spaces, or stigma.

Can conserve spoons by making movement less physically expensive.
Can provide a wide stable base for balance and lower-extremity impairment, reducing falls and improving short-distance confidence.

entries.name: Standard Walker
entries.source_excerpt: er balance and stability. There are a few types of walkers available including: Standard Walker: A walker with four nonskid, rubber-tipped legs to provide stability. It must be picked up to be moved. ✔ Two-Wheel Walker: This type of walker has wheels on the front two legs and is helpful if you need some, but not constant, weight-bearing help. ✔ Three-Wheel Walker: This walker provides the user with balance support like a four-wheel walker, but it’s lighter and more maneuverable. ✔  6/12/26, 5:32 AM Your Complete Mobility Guide: A Sim

entries.name: Rollator

possible_life_improvements_or_supports: Can improve safety, independence, comfort, confidence, and participation by reducing falls, pain, fatigue, or distance barriers.
Can conserve spoons by making movement less physically expensive.
Can allow a quicker, more natural gait and often provides a seat/storage for pacing.

entries.name: Knee Walker
entries.source_excerpt: r: This is ideal for people who don’t need to lean on the walker for balance. ✔ Knee Walker: A knee walker is similar to a foot-propelled scooter, but it has a platform for resting your knee. ✔ Three-Wheel Rollator: These rollators fit in smaller spaces, can make sharp turns and have increased maneuverability. They’re lighter than the four-wheeled options, however, their design means they don’t offer a seat. They have one swiveling wheel in the front and two wheels in the back. They often come with a basket, pouch or both for storage.

These rollators have two swiveling wheels in the front and two wheels in the back. They come with seats that may be padded.
